data.title: LightPath Technologies: A Lot Of Promise, But The Sector Seems Overpriced
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to take a look at LightPath’s (LPTH) meteoric share price increase over the last year to see if it was warranted and still a good time to get in. Operations are improving, and the broader outlook seems to be promising; however, the company’s valuation seems to be stretched right now, making me think the growth is already priced in.

The Company

LightPath Technologies is a vertically integrated provider of advanced optics and imaging systems, with a primary focus on defense and commercial markets. The company designs and manufactures custom optical components, assemblies, and infrared cameras and provides engineering services. The components are used in products like thermal imaging and sensing applications for defense, industrial, and commercial sectors. Integrated lens assemblies and camera modules that combine optics with sensors and mechanics, which are highly sought after in the most modern applications, like drones, industrial inspection and automation, and advanced driver-assistance systems.

Briefly on Financials

The company’s sales completely transformed around the end of 2024. 2025 has been a fantastic year for the company as its sales got a second wind.

We can see that two of the company’s four segments experienced significant growth over the year, specifically the Infrared components and Assemblies, and modules. In the most recent earnings report, we can see that these two saw a growth of 63% and a whopping 435%, respectively, helping the company reach an overall y/y growth of around 80% for the first quarter. The massive jump in both of the segments mentioned is not due to organic growth, however, but more due to the company’s acquisition of G5 Infrared, so it is not as good as it looks, but it represents a massive boost to the company’s operations.

Of course, such explosive growth doesn’t come cheap. The company’s margins across the board have taken a hit over the last two years or so, with operating margins and net margins being considerably in the red for a while now. On the plus side, we can see that EBIT margins have somewhat improved from the recent lows but are still quite in the red.

Looking at the latest earnings report, gross margins declined by 400 bps due to “certain nonrecurring or end-of-life orders in the prior year that had higher margins.”

Operating expenses grew 66%, with the increase due to the integration expenses of G5’s acquisition. The management pointed to an improvement on a non-GAAP basis by singling out adjusted EBITDA turning positive at around $360K.

On some other important metrics, the company’s backlog grew to around $90m, representing around three years’ revenues. The backlog gives us visibility and certainty, and we like certainty. Backlog grew around 5% y/y, which isn’t particularly exciting but is growth nonetheless.

Let’s have a look at the company’s financial position. LightPath finished the quarter with around $11.5m in cash and equivalents against around $4.8m in long-term debt. Is this worrisome, considering the company is not profitable? I hardly think so. The company’s cash pile can easily cover the debt outstanding. It is important to note that the company’s cash position has improved considerably since June 2025 due to the recent $8m private equity placement. The management has acknowledged the continued negative GAAP performance but also stressed that the cash burn is decreasing and the company has sufficient liquidity to fund ongoing operations, working capital needs, and strategic initiatives.

This view is supported by the company’s ability to turn positive on adjusted EBITDA, as well as an increase in cash from operating activities to -$1.1m from -$1.7m. So, I think the company will be operating for much longer, and I don’t see it going out of business anytime soon. I’m sure further equity financing may be required, which will dilute shareholders, but if the company’s share price continues to skyrocket, that is not going to be an issue.

Overall, the company’s recent M&A action worked out well in terms of boosting its top line growth considerably; however, it remains to be seen if this is going to be sustained going forward. I believe that once the acquisition makes a full year, we are not going to see such explosive growth, unless there is decent organic growth in the future, in terms of an increase in backlog and new orders. The backlog growing by around 5% is what I would expect to see in the future in the company’s top-line growth.

Comments on the Outlook

Let’s focus on the two mentioned business sectors and why I think they will continue to provide decent growth going forward.

Defense and Aerospace- the key growth sectors

With the recent successful acquisition of G5, the company will continue to see a lot of growth here due to the Defense and Aerospace sectors being very hot right now. These sectors are the largest and fastest-growing sectors for the company, which represent around 70% of total revenues. Key applications here include thermal imaging systems, weapon sights, surveillance, and space and border security.

The global aerospace and defense fiber optic cables market alone looks very promising with a CAGR of 8%-10% through 2034, driven by the adoption of durable fiber optics in defense aircraft and space programs.

Furthermore, governments across the globe are increasing defense spending, which means there should be a lot more money around for companies like LPTH. Due to the conflicts all around the globe, like in Ukraine and Israel, many governments are looking to expand their defense programs with advanced autonomous technology, like UAVs, surveillance satellites, and drones.

Drone operations have been growing a lot and have shown a lot of success in Ukraine, so it is a very promising growth segment for LTPH. Conflict is, of course, not what anyone wants to see, and I would prefer a company not profiting from such a disaster, but it is unavoidable. It is a double-edged sword for the company. If global tensions escalate, the company will most likely see a decent uptick in sales, but that also comes with a supply chain disruption. The recent geopolitical conflicts put some stress on the company’s ability to procure germanium, as China has a dominant global position in the production and processing. China implemented export controls and eventually banned germanium exports to the US due to security concerns. Germanium is a critical component in infrared optics, like thermal imaging and night vision. So, this shortage raised prices and eroded many companies’ profitability.

How is the company looking to bypass this issue? It aims to significantly reduce dependence on germanium via solid innovation like its proprietary BlackDiamond glass. This is a specialized chalcogenide glass developed to be a more abundant, cost-effective, and domestic alternative to germanium, which offers similar or even better performance and mitigates the past supply chain issues. The company is actively transitioning from germanium, but it has still not fully done so, so germanium remains an important material.

The company recently signed contracts with Ondas Holdings (ONDS) and Unusual Machines (UMAC), both of which are leading providers of autonomous robotics and NDAA-compliant drone components, which will accelerate LPTH’s deployment of its advanced optics and thermal imaging in drones that are used for defense, government, and commercial applications.

Valuation

With such an unprofitable situation right now, doing a DCF analysis is not a good way to find what the company is worth, so I decided to do a comps analysis instead. The company, according to its 10-K report, faces competition from start to finish of the product and mentions MKS Instruments’ (MKSI) subsidiary Ophir Optronics Solutions, Teledyne FLIR (TDY), RTX Corp (RTX), and Elbit Systems (ESLT), among others that are not currently trading on the stock exchanges. It’s kind of a limited list, so I’m adding a few extra to my comparison model, but just so you are aware, they may not be perfect substitutes. Below is the list settled on.

We can see that most of the companies are trading at quite high multiples across the board, which isn’t a good start for the company right now. Additionally, the valuation won’t be very accurate since some companies do not have positive earnings and EBITDA like our target company, so according to valuation by multiples, we only get to see comparisons through P/S ratio, P/B ratio, and EV/Revenue.

Since LPTH demands the highest multiples out of the ones available, the valuation tells us that the company is quite overvalued. Taking these multiples into account, we get the company’s intrinsic value below, telling us LPTH is quite overvalued.

This can change if and when the company begins to make a profit on the bottom line, but for now, the separate multiples are clearly telling us LPTH is overvalued.

Closing Comments

Am I looking to invest? It does look quite promising, given the ever-increasing need for AI and autonomous machines in conflicts; however, I would like to see what the growth looks like once the inorganic growth phase passes over the next two quarters. I expect the growth to slow down considerably, and I’m not sure I can see the company becoming profitable any time soon, unless operations improve drastically, which they haven’t as of the latest quarter. The company’s share price skyrocketed this past year, which already makes it a bit less attractive. To me, looks like the aggressive growth was already priced in.

Nevertheless, I am putting the company on my watchlist and will revisit whenever I get more information on its growth potential.

data.title: The Charles Schwab Corporation: Rate Cuts Are Not A Problem (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of The Charles Schwab Corporation (SCHW) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 11%. Given the importance of cash economics to its business, shares have softened as the Fed has begun its rate cutting cycle. However, I actually see the company as positioned fairly well for a moderate cutting cycle. I last covered Schwab in August, rating the stock a “hold,” and since then, shares have lost about 5% while the market has added 6%, suggesting even a “sell” rating was merited. With shares seemingly range bound and further potential Fed rate cuts on the horizon, now is a good time to revisit SCHW. I am more bullish.

Forge Purchase Makes Strategic Sense

In early November, Schwab announced a deal to buy Forge Global (FRGE) for $660 million. While a relatively small deal, SCHW paid a large premium of 72% (I view premiums above 40% as being unusually high). This suggests there were likely several interested parties. Plus the premium basically amounts to ~$0.25 per SCHW share, so even if it overpaid a bit, the overpayment is not particularly material to SCHW relative to the strategic purpose of the acquisition.

Namely, Forge operates a marketplace of private investments, an area where Schwab is lacking exposure. By integrating Forge into its Alternative Investments platform, Schwab will make it easier for its advisors and clients to invest in private securities, an increasing focus especially with eased regulatory rules for these securities in 401(K) retirement plans.

Forge has 3 million users and 625 private companies listed on its platform, with about $17 billion of transactions. Frankly, Forge is not going to be a material direct contributor to SCHW’s results. Rather, the purpose of this acquisition is to immediately scale a private securities platform rather than building one from scratch with SCHW taking this platform and gradually rolling it across its customer base, including launching a fund to add AUM. With growing demand for private securities, this acquisition ensures that Schwab maintains a leading-edge product offering to continue accumulating client assets.

Asset Gathering Has Been Strong

Right now, the company is excelling on that front. In mid-November, Schwab provided a monthly update on assets. Net new assets were an excellent $138 billion, up 44% from last year, as the company continues to win market share. That brings client assets to $11.8 trillion, up 20% from last year thanks to both fund flows and market appreciation. This strong NNA performance continues a trend we have seen, with Q3 coming in 44% above last year’s level at $138 billion.

In October, highly lucrative margin loans were up 7% sequentially, and with sizable market volatility, trading remains elevated at 8.6 million daily trades. I was encouraged to see cash sweep balances rise by $3.2 billion to $429 billion, primarily due to typical seasonal trends. Cash utilization was down to 9.3% from 9.8% a year ago. With rates relatively elevated, we have seen customers carry less cash to invest in higher yielding money market funds and short-dated fixed income. This had been a source of pressure on Schwab’s earnings, but with much of this played out, earnings growth has been excellent.

In the company’s third quarter, Charles Schwab earned $1.31, which was up 70% from last year, as revenue grew 27% to $6.1 billion. Earnings were also $0.06 ahead of consensus. Operating margins continue to be best-in-class at 51.3%. This was up by 10.1% last year as net interest recovered, speaking to the tremendous operating leverage in the business. While Schwab grew trading and advisory fees, net interest revenue was the standout at $3.1 billion, and this remains the core driver of the company’s profitability.

Net Interest Trends Are Favorable

In Q3, Schwab paid down more expensive wholesale funding by $13 billion, which should continue to support net interest margins. Schwab, like most cash sweep custodians, began to see balance pressure intensify after the failure of Silicon Valley Bank in early 2023, as that failure raised the issue of how much cash was sitting in low-yielding accounts. As Schwab saw clients reduce their cash positions, it was forced to rely on more expensive wholesale funding, reaching a peak of nearly $100 billion. Over time, as its business has grown, Schwab has been able to work down this expensive funding, bringing wholesale funding back to the upper-end of its $5-$15 billion target.

In Q3, Schwab’s NIM expanded 78bps to 2.86%. Its funding costs fell to 85bps from 1.61%, primarily due to lower wholesale funding as well as pass-through of the Fed rate cuts. This will likely migrate a bit lower with Fed rate cuts, but just by ~20-30% as much as Fed rate cuts vs the ~75% beta so far.

On the other side of its balance sheet, asset yields actually improved 2bps to 3.71%, as increased securities lending revenue and favorable mix shift offset lower yields on floating rate securities. Like many banks. Schwab is saddled with fixed-rate securities bought when rates were lower. It has ~$207 billion of securities at a ~1.8% yield. As these mature, SCHW can reinvest them at higher market yields. With a ~4 year duration on these securities, this will be a prolonged process.

Assuming ~3-4 Fed rate cuts over the next year, I expect the benefits of fixed reinvestment to offset the negatives on floating-rate assets and leave its portfolio’s yield roughly flat. If the Fed cuts more than 4 times, we likely would see the portfolio yield fall somewhat, and with fewer cuts, we would see the portfolio yield rise. With funding costs likely to fall somewhat further, this means that SCHW’s NIM should continue to expand and likely surpass 3% by mid-2026, which will help support incremental earnings.

Shares Are Now A Buy

Additionally, Schwab maintains a 9.7% regulatory tier 1 leverage ratio, flat to last year and well above regulatory minimums, providing ample capacity for buybacks, dividends, and bolt-on M&A like the Forge deal. Given its excellent capital position, Schwab bought back $2.7 billion of stock in the third quarter. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 1.3% from last year. Shares also pay a 1.2% dividend yield.

Back in August, I was targeting about $4.60-$5.00 in earnings over the next year. Since then, the market has moved a bit higher, supporting increased fee-based revenue. With the passage of time, we also continue to move into higher earning periods as NIM should steadily expand at least through the next 12 months, and Schwab’s balance sheet reshaping has progressed a bit more quickly than I hoped. In calendar 2026, I am looking for the company to earn $5.35-$5.70, depending on cash balance trends and assuming the S&P 500 remains around current levels. This assumes no material impact from Forge. While I believe the deal makes clear strategic sense, I do not expect a material impact on results for 18+ months.

Shares are now below 16.5x as its multiple has compressed notably. That comes even as I expect SCHW’s NIM to expand modestly over the next year, leaving SCHW relatively well-positioned for Fed rate cuts. Plus, lower rates may also reduce pressure on cash balances, further supporting earning. For a company with organic growth or 4-5% plus the benefits of market appreciation, I view this as an attractive multiple, as those factors support combined earnings growth of 8-10%. As such, I am upgrading shares to a “buy” and see shares passing $100 or 18x earnings, reflecting its growing core business and favorable NIM trends.

data.title: Waste Management: Stericycle Acquisition Is A Winner But Not Enough At This Valuation
data.description:
Q3 2025 Earnings Takeaway

For Q3 2025, Waste Management, Inc.(WM) delivered a revenue of $6.44 billion and an EBITDA of $1.718 billion, up by around 15% and 2.44% YoY, respectively.

Adjusting for one-time expenses from Stericycle integration costs and impairment charge from the temporary operation shutdown in one of WM's recycling businesses, adjusted EBITDA is $1.970 billion, up by 15% YoY. Free cash flow generation for the quarter is $821 million, up from $618 million last year.

Turning over to segmental performance, which is WM labeled as Legacy Businesses, consisting of its Collection and Disposal, Energy, and Recycling, and WM Healthcare Solutions, formerly known as Stericycle.

WM Legacy Business revenue is $5.815 billion, up 3.7% YoY. WM Legacy growth is lower than last year's due to the drag from WM's recycling business. According to the management, recycled commodity prices declined by almost 35%. The introduction of automation and technology managed to offset this commodity decline, which led to the recycling business's EBITDA growing by 18%.

Last year's acquisition, Stericycle, now WM Healthcare Solutions, delivered a revenue of $628 million with an EBITDA of $89 million. Excluding integration costs such as severance, retention costs, and ERP implementation, WM Healthcare's adjusted EBITDA is around $110 million, with an EBITDA margin of 17.5%.

This is a quarterly good result for WM. Despite the maturity of the core legacy business and the cyclical headwind of its commodity segment, the company continues to find ways to expand profitability through introduction of automation and technology to improve the company's efficiency and productivity. WM's core disposal and collection business is very stable, considering the significant barriers to entry in this business. An investor could extrapolate from its historical growth rate with certainty. Most of WM's market value comes from this business.

However, what I'm interested in is the latest significant strategic move that WM has made, which is the Stericycle acquisition. This business segment will be a key growth area for WM and, as will be detailed below, will likely deliver value creation for shareholders.

Stericycle Historical Brief Takeaway

On November 4, 2025, WM completed its acquisition of Stericycle for an enterprise value of $7.2 billion. At first, when assessing the acquisition, I'm skeptical about the acquisition. Before the acquisition, Stericycle had been on a clear financial decline for multiple years, which appears to be secular in nature.

The decline has been attributed to the consolidation of hospitals, which increases their bargaining power due to increased size and scale. This customer consolidation has negatively affected Stericycle's pricing power. Additionally, Stericycle has undertaken poor M&A decisions in the past, resulting in excessive debt and poor integration outcomes, which exacerbated the problem.

Stericycle uses specialized equipment and assets to collect medical waste, which is more complex to collect than traditional waste. This specialized equipment and procedure for collecting medical waste entail a higher cost than the traditional waste that WM has catered to. However, after assessing the cost synergies the combined enterprise could realize and the potential of applying WM operational know-how into Stericycle, I believe that this is a very good acquisition for WM.

Cost Synergy Alone Makes The Stericycle Acquisition Value Accretive To Shareholders

But first, let's analyze the numbers involved in the Stericycle acquisition.

Using Stericycle's 2024 TTM EBITDA of around $400 million and an acquisition enterprise value of $7.2 billion, this translates to an acquisition EV/EBITDA multiple of around 18x, above WM's EV/EBITDA multiple around the acquisition. In any M&A, an acquirer needs to pay a premium to entice the acquiree's shareholders to sell. However, the need to pay a price premium is the usual bane of any M&A transaction, as the premium paid is often excessive relative to the potential synergy value that could be realized.

To calculate the potential value creation of an M&A transaction, I use the following formula: M&A Value Creation = Value Of The Synergy - Premium Paid.

Remember this formula, as we will get back to this later. Now, how much premium did WM pay for the Stericycle acquisition? To calculate the premium, we multiply Stericycle's 2024 EBITDA of around $400 million by WM's EV/EBITDA multiple around the acquisition, which is around 15x. This will translate to around $6 billion enterprise value for Stericycle. At an acquisition enterprise value of $7.2 billion, the premium is around $1.2 billion.

This constitutes a premium of around 20%. Coincidentally, 20% is also the premium that WM paid for Stericycle's shareholders. But what's the rationale for using this kind of method?

Often, the stock market's judgment of a particular stock is reflective of its real value. Using the market leader's valuation multiple, in this case WM, is often a representative of that industry's fundamentals and potential.

Following the completion of Stericycle's acquisition, the management projected a run-rate cost synergy exceeding $125 million, with $80 million to $100 million expected to be achieved by the end of 2025. The company, in its investor day, would later double this run-rate cost synergy from $125 million to $250 million by 2027.

The initial run-rate cost synergy alone pretty much justifies this deal. One method to calculate if the premium was justified is to divide the run-rate cost synergy by the combined enterprise's cost of capital or discount rate. The purpose of the calculation is to determine the value to perpetuity of the synergy. For strong companies like WM, I simply subtract the minimum return that investors should demand for an equity investment, usually around 8% to 10%, by the most likely future US GDP growth of around 2% to 3%. Then I compare it to the risk-free rate and subjectively determine the appropriate discount rate based on my qualitative assessment. Determining the appropriate discount rate is more of an art than a science. I always demand a discount rate higher than a risk-free rate, as no matter how strong the company's fundamentals, there will always be extra risk associated with an equity investment. I believe WM's cost of capital is around 7% to 8%. Now it is at the investor's whim to arbitrarily settle for a lower cost of capital of, say, 6% to 7%, after his own diligence, but I prefer to be conservative, so I will settle for a higher one.

By dividing the run-rate cost savings of around $125 million by 8%, the value to perpetuity of the cost synergy should be around $1.56 billion. Clearly, there is an excess of $360 million. This excess value is the value creation of the acquisition.

Moreover, suppose the management achieved the revised run-rate cost savings it foresees by 2027 of around $250 million, the acquisition value creation will be around $1.925 billion.

Can an investor assume that this cost synergy is attainable? After all, these are all future assumptions, which are always uncertain, especially in the heavily regulated business of WM.

I believe it is. The justification for a premium price paid in an acquisition comes from two sources: cost and revenue synergy. Ideally, an investor should prefer an acquisition premium that is justified mostly by cost synergy, or even better solely by it.

Reducing costs is always a more controllable endeavor than realizing revenue synergy, which often involves cross-selling that relies on unpredictable demand. A simple question to ask when assessing the revenue synergy presented by a management in an acquisition is, 'Will customers value the products sold by the combined enterprise?' I definitely see a cross-selling opportunity for WM, like offering solid waste management service to Stericycle's existing customers. However, I still prefer that the justification for a premium is mainly from cost synergy.

Aside from the generic removal of redundant SG&A function, cost synergy can be realized from the increased scale economics of logistics functions such as fleet repairs and maintenance, and Stericycle gaining access to WM's operational know-how.

According to WM's Head of Investor Relations Ed Egl, a significant portion of the $250 million cost synergy WM has forecasted will come from SG&A.

And then on top of that, we double our synergies from 125 to 250, right? A lot of that's going to be coming from SG&A, and that's going to bring their SG&A roughly to about 15%, 16% of revenue. As you know, today, we're running around 9%, right? So in three years from now, there's probably more opportunity to get that SG&A a little bit lower. None of these synergies contemplate cross-selling opportunities as well, right? There's an additional revenue opportunities here.

WM management's progress on this matter has been significant. And it is evident in the chart below, where the SG&A intensity(SG&A / Revenue) of Stericycle shows a substantial decline since the acquisition.

Clearly, with the combination, Stericycle has been able to leverage WM's corporate overhead function. And more importantly, Stericycle has gained access to the more intangible capability of WM, such as its culture, process, and best practices. WM management sees this declining to 17% in the future and judging the trend, I believe it is reasonable to assume that this target is achievable.

Lastly, Stericycle will benefit from WM's access to cheap cost of capital due to strong cash generation and access to cheap debt.

The Importance Of An Optimal Capital Structure For WM

I believe one of the most important factors that an investor needs to assess when investing in a mature but stable company, such as WM, is an optimal capital structure. When a business is stable and has strong profitability or cash flow generation, it should carry more debt, and if it's cyclical and rapidly changing, then more equity with little to no debt. The ultimate objective of an optimal capital structure is to maximize the productivity of the company's equity without disregarding the requirements of the business. Additionally, an optimal capital structure should lessen WM's cost of capital due to efficient use of debt and equity as debt is often cheaper than equity, which is crucial to WM's future acquisitions. The acquiree would be able to leverage WM's cheap cost of capital to fund its expenditures.

When considering the inherent stability and cash flow generation of WM, it has the capability to have a more aggressive capital structure.

Additionally, an optimal capital structure is often indicative of the quality of the management's capital allocation. A company with sound capital allocation returns excess capital when it makes sense to do so. As mentioned earlier, an optimal capital structure allows a more productive use of equity capital, especially among mature and profitable companies, due to efficient use of leverage. This is evident in the chart below. Despite earning a return on capital employed of around 10%, which, I believe, is average, the company was able to earn an impressive return on equity.

WM is one of those unique cases that, despite the seemingly capped ability of the business to generate a high return on capital employed, the inherent stability of the business model enables the company to use more debt to make its equity more productive. This is common among regulated industries. The force of regulation limits a company's ability to generate a high return on its capital employed. However, the very same forces of regulation itself may provide the stability to the business through increased barriers to entry that enable adoption of more debt in the balance sheet. For WM, the complex regulatory environment, heavy capital intensity, and the high minimum efficient scale provide significant barriers to entry that give stability, as proven by its long periods of financial and operational stability.

Valuation

WM is a company that can be categorized as a mature and low-growth company. Ideally, you want these types of mature companies to possess the following attributes: a high free cash flow conversion rate, consistently high returns on equity capital, efficient use of leverage, and a consistent dividend payer or frequently buy back their shares. In my opinion, this kind of company attracts value-oriented investors rather than growth ones. And WM fits all these attributes. However, another crucial factor is the price an investor should pay, as paying a growth-like valuation for a mature company can hinder decent returns for an investor. Historically, WM's revenue and EBITDA have grown at a CAGR of around 6.2% and 7.6%, respectively.

While respectable growth for a mature company, it doesn't justify WM's P/E multiple of around 34x, which is slightly higher than the S&P 500's current P/E of around 30x. This P/E ratio translates to around 3.12% earnings yield, which is a premium when compared to the long-term risk-free rate of around 4.7%(US 30Y bond yield) and doesn't compensate enough for the extra risk that is always present in equities. Additionally, relative to its historical EV/EBIT multiple, WM is currently selling on the upper end of its historical multiple.

Now, one may argue that good companies should sell at a higher valuation as it reflects the quality of the company. Quality companies are those that possess stability, high returns on capital, and cash generation, which I believe WM possesses. After all, the judgment of the market often reflects the true value of a particular stock. I sometimes agree with this statement. However, an investor needs to always consider the price he is paying. Paying a high price for a quality company exposes the investor to the risk of underperformance due to the embedded high expectation from the market, which makes it harder and harder for the company to outperform this expectation. An investor shouldn't think that the market's judgment is wrong, but only that he disagrees with it. It's not about being right or wrong about the future, but rather an investor positioning himself in scenarios where the odds of generating an adequate return are in his favor. Paying for a growth-like valuation for a mature company doesn't put an investor in a position where the odds are in his favor. Therefore, I will call a 'Hold' rating for this stock.

data.title: SPHD: Equity-Like Returns With More Stability
data.description:
Invesco S&P 500® High Dividend Low Volatility ETF (SPHD) labels itself as offering high dividends and low volatility. With the bearish market of late, one might wonder if this fund is ideal, should these conditions persist.

I don't think SPHD meets the income potential implied by the "High Dividend" part, but it does seem likely to provide the usual returns of an equity fund, with the benefit of relative stability.

Concept Of The Fund

SPHD is a passively managed equity fund. According to the prospectus, it's something of an index within an index:

...designed to measure the performance of approximately 50 securities in the S&P 500 ® Index that historically have provided high dividend yields while exhibiting lower volatility.

It is therefore not an equity index that searches the entire market for equities that provide low volatility and high yields. Rather, it sources only from the constituents of the S&P 500 and seeks fifty investments that it most meet these criteria.

This means each holding typically make up about 1% to 3% of the portfolio.

The sample above of some of their larger holdings, with recognizable large caps like Pfizer (PFE), Altria (MO), and UPS (UPS).

These, plus a look at the sector breakdown, reveal a portfolio that is very diversified with this is approach, notably lacking the same kind of tech focus for which the S&P 500 is known in more recent years. Given the aim for low volatility, this isn't surprising, as tech stocks, which have had significant earnings and revenue growth in recent years, are known for volatility.

Historical Results

The question, then, is if this approach historically meets the goal of high dividends and low volatility.

Looking at the past ten years, SPHD has managed to provide monthly income. The end result is one of dividend increases, with the monthly distribution being $0.09 per share in January 2015, with the most recent being $0.19. Nevertheless, it's also worth noting the ups and downs, with distributions rising and falling throughout this period as well.

If one bought ten years ago or during the COVID crash of March 2020, they would have a yield on cost near 6%, but in general, it's more of a moderate yield than what I would consider a high one.

The past decade of total returns show some interesting trends. Prior to COVID, it largely outperformed the S&P. During COVID, when the Index shifted toward growing tech stocks, SPHD was overtaken. In 2022, when the market turned bearish in the face of both the Federal Reserve's aggressive rate hikes and macro effects of the Russian invasion of Ukraine, it nearly caught up.

Tech's gains related to AI over the past two years have widened the gap once again, but the narrowing of the gap when the market turns bearish does show that the fund can hold up well when there is pessimism.

Limited to S&P 500 stocks, SPHD it may not be the best income vehicle historically, but the fund has at least sorted well to manage the volatility.

Outlook And Risks

The current dividend yield is only about 3.77%, so an income thesis would depend on strong growth of this dividend. History shows that this is a challenge for them. As they lack the growth elements of the Index, there isn't much for dividend growth from their underlying companies beyond those from gradual expansion and price increases for their products. I don't think I could recommend this fund for that. Moreover, temporary dividend reductions could disrupt an investor's income situation, and this fund has a history of that.

I can imagine a total returns thesis, in which an index investor believes the market is in a bubble but doesn't want to move into cash. SPHD could serve as an equity-based alternative to cash, providing a yield fairly close to a money market, with more growth on the yield and the capital gains than a money market can offer. Nevertheless, this would not completely shield investors in the event of a true market crash.

Averaged over time, SPHD seems likely to provide positive but fairly normal returns from an equity index, in the upper-single digits. The main advantage would be if one thinks the broader market is on a bearish path for much longer than the last two months. Of course, if one is okay with average returns over time, what does volatility matter?

Conclusion

SPHD is a tool that functions as advertised, but I don't see an advantage that would warrant a general Buy rating at this time. Instead, it seems better for specific investors or portfolio managers who think its features fit their strategy better. Lacking a bad record, I don't see cause for too much worry, either. For that reason, as ETFs go, I think SPHD is a reasonable Hold.

data.title: Hartford Small Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities surged in the third quarter, registering a sizable return year to date. Technology and small-cap stocks realized significant gains, and growth stocks outpaced their value counterparts by a wide margin. Lower trade-policy uncertainty, robust economic growth, and much stronger-than-anticipated second-quarter corporate earnings fueled the equity rally despite concerns about inflation, tariffs, and a cooling labor market.

The Russell 2000 Value Index returned 12.60% for the period. Within the Index, all 11 sectors rose over the period. Communication services, materials, and information technology were the top performing sectors, while consumer staples and financials were the bottom performing sectors.

Performance Summary

  • The Hartford Small Cap Value Fund (I share) underperformed the Russell 2000 Value Index during the quarter; however, the Fund delivered overall positive returns.
  • Security selection drove relative underperformance. Weak selection in industrials, information technology, and materials was partially offset by stronger selection in consumer staples, consumer discretionary, and real estate.
  • Sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock selection process, also detracted from relative results, primarily due to our overweight exposure to financials, and underweights to communication services and healthcare. This was partially offset by our underweight exposure to real estate and overweight to materials, which contributed.
  • The top individual contributors to relative performance over the period included overweight positions in American Eagle Outfitters (AEO)(consumer discretionary) and WK Kellogg (KLG) (consumer staples); the top detractors included an overweight position in Cracker Barrel (CBRL)(consumer discretionary) and an out-of-benchmark position in Kemper (financials).

Positioning & Outlook

  • The management team has been active in identifying undervalued companies with superior capital allocation and quality that often become mispriced by the market.
  • We believe that the small-cap value category could offer the most attractive long-term total return potential for shareholders. Due to the lower level of institutional coverage and greater level of stock volatility, we believe in a process-oriented approach that focuses on high return areas and leverages information and analysis to differentiate between value traps and value opportunities.
  • At the end of the period, consumer discretionary and financials represented our largest sector overweights relative to the benchmark, while energy and real estate were our largest underweights.

data.title: Harmony Gold: Playing The Peer Catch-Up Trade As Gold Finds Its Zen (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since I last covered Harmony Gold (NYSE:HMY) with a Hold rating, shares are up 58.14% on a total return basis vs 12.59% for the S&P 500.

Quarterly Trends

I have laid out 20 quarters of production trends to give readers a clear sense of how Harmony Gold has been performing over the last five years. Harmony remains primarily a South African gold producer, with most of its ounces coming from a blend of deep-level underground mines and a handful of open-pit operations. Harmony's only gold-producing asset outside of South Africa is the Hidden Valley mine in Papua New Guinea. On the copper side, Harmony has also steadily been building its presence. It owns the Eva Copper Project in Queensland, Australia—a large open-pit copper-gold development with the potential for a 15-year mine life. More recently, the acquisition of the CSA copper mine through MAC Copper gives Harmony its first cash-flowing copper operation, signaling a genuine shift toward becoming a multi-commodity mining company. Normally, I am wary of miners branching into new commodities, but I am very bullish on copper over the long term, so I actually like this move—and I appreciate the fact that Harmony is making hay while the sun shines, taking advantage of current profits in gold while times are good, and using them to invest in copper, a metal that I see as crucial for the future.

In the Q1 2026 Earnings Report, management explained their strategy best:

Our strategy remains gold-first, with copper as a complementary metal. We continue to unlock value through reserve conversion and disciplined, value-accretive mergers and acquisitions. Harmony’s 136Moz Mineral Resource and 37Moz Mineral Reserve base support a stable gold production profile of 1.4Moz to 1.5Moz annually for over 20 years. Reserve conversion opportunities, especially at current gold prices, offer further upside. The integration of CSA mine and advancement of Eva Copper will strengthen medium-term resilience – timeously mitigating the Moab Khotsong ore gap. Pending the approval of Eva Copper, copper production of approximately 100 000 tonnes per year is expected within three to five years, enhancing margins and diversifying cash flow.

Metrics For All Operations

Production trends and company-wide average gold grades over the last 20 quarters have been fairly consistent and pretty solid.

These solid production trends as well as relatively stable cost control—albeit costs that are slightly higher than the industry-wide average—have resulted in Harmony fully benefiting from higher gold prices in the last three years, allowing margins to skyrocket. This past quarter, ending September 2025, was the highest margin quarter ever.

Harmony isn't just sitting back and enjoying this windfall. They have been actively managing their hedge book, rolling maturing contracts into zero-cost collars that cover 10%-30% of production over the next three years. That's smart—it locks in cash flow on a portion of ounces while leaving plenty of exposure to further upside. I think it is the kind of pragmatic and disciplined approach that will give the company room to fund projects without over-stretching the balance sheet. The flip side of that is that with the gold price surging, Harmony has naturally lagged some unhedged peers. While I respect the discipline and intent to protect the balance sheet and maintain funding optionality, it does mean that investors have to accept that part of the rally will naturally be left on the table.

South Africa Underground Operations

South Africa underground operations account for the bulk of Harmony's production—and were responsible for 71.55% of production this past quarter. This is also the segment with the highest AISC.

As deeper ore is accessed, extraction becomes more complex, energy-intensive, and costly, which naturally drives up All-In Sustaining Costs. I think that maintaining production levels while controlling costs for this segment of Harmony's operations will continue to be an ongoing balancing act.

South Africa Surface Operations

Gold production from South Africa surface operations accounted for 16.09% of production this past quarter. However, AISC has been rapidly rising for this segment as well.

Hidden Valley (Papua New Guinea)

Hidden Valley accounted for 12.36% of gold production this past quarter—and is currently the lowest-cost segment in Harmony's portfolio. However, as you can see in the three graphs below, production and cost metrics have been fairly volatile. Hidden Valley is an underground and open-pit hybrid operation with highly variable ore zones—which makes production planning challenging, leading to volatile production and cost trends.

Project Updates

Eva Copper

The company provided a short update in Q1 2026 Earnings, saying that Harmony's Eva Copper Project in northwest Queensland continues to make steady progress. Early site works are already underway, supported by a conditional grant from the Queensland government. The feasibility study update is nearing completion, and engineering and design work is moving forward. HMY is engaging with the state government to secure a power solution that meets the project's requirements while fitting into Queensland's long-term energy strategy. The project's current mineral resources indicate 482Mt of copper ore at 0.40%—around 1.93Mt of contained copper and 196Mt of gold ore at 0.07 g/t—roughly 440koz of contained gold. Harmony's board is expected to make a final investment decision once the feasibility update is complete—a process anticipated to be completed before the end of the current calendar year.

If all goes well, Eva Copper is expected to add 100,000 tonnes of copper per year (within three to five years from now). I think this is particularly exciting considering the copper prices are currently sitting near all-time highs. Furthermore, I expect copper prices to continue rising, as the copper market falls deeper into a structural deficit in the years to come.

Wafi-Golpu

In Q1 2026 Earnings, management didn't give investors much to go on regarding this project but remarked:

Discussions between Harmony, our joint venture partner Newmont Corporation and the Papua New Guinea Government to secure the special mining lease are continuing.

Risks

While Harmony's portfolio looks solid, there are a few risks that investors still need to keep in mind. South African underground operations make up the bulk of production, and deeper ore means higher costs and more complex extraction. Keeping All-In Sustaining Costs in check will require careful management—something that I don't think management has excelled in if looking at recent AISC trends. Surface operations and the Hidden Valley project provide some diversification, but they also have shown some volatility in production and costs.

On the development side, projects like Eva Copper and Wafi-Golpu carry execution, permitting, and energy-supply risks, and any delays there could materially impact expected cash flows.

Finally, currency risks also matter. While revenues are earned in Dollars, Harmony reports in ZAR (South African Rand); therefore, it prefers a weaker ZAR relative to the Dollar (with costs also being incurred in ZAR). The Rand has strengthened quite a bit against the Dollar this year. If that trend continues, it will be a drag on Harmony's share price.

Valuation

For my relative valuation, I have benchmarked Harmony Gold against peers that include Newmont (NEM), Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), Barrick (B), Gold Fields (GFI), AngloGold (AU), Kinross (KGC), and Centerra Gold (CGAU). I selected these peers because I think their scale provides a meaningful basis for comparing operational performance and valuation.

I prefer using FWD EV/EBITDA ratios when valuing mining companies, as this tells me what I am paying for the underlying operations relative to the earnings they are forecast to generate.

Over the past two years, Harmony Gold traded at an FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 21% below the peer group, on average. Currently, Harmony is trading at a ratio that is 38% below the peer group—suggesting that shares are currently undervalued on a relative basis when compared against peers.

Harmony's FWD EV/EBITDA ratio currently sits 1.9 standard deviations below its 2-year average compared to the peer group—suggesting that now might be a decent entry point.

Notably, Harmony Gold has lagged all its peers over the past year, contributing to the relative undervaluation described above. I think a big part of that comes down to its hedging policy—investors have been gravitating toward the unhedged names as gold prices surged. But a lot of that is already baked into Harmony's share price, and I think there is room for catch-up. If gold takes a breather or slips back below $4,000, I would expect Harmony to hold up better than many of its peers.

Analyst Ratings

A slim majority of Wall Street Analysts that publish their ratings on the Bloomberg Terminal have a Buy rating on shares of Harmony—with a 12-month consensus price target that is 23% above the current share price.

The Technical Take

Shares of Harmony Gold appear to be entering a consolidation phase—albeit with a slightly bearish bias over the near term. HMY is trading below its 20-day and 50-day moving average but is doing a good job of holding above the 200-day moving average. The $19 mark on the chart looks to be an area of resistance—and I would like to see Harmony break through that level for confirmation that the long-term bullish trend is still intact.

Takeaway

I am upgrading Harmony Gold to a Buy primarily because the stock has been lagging peers for reasons that I don't think are durable over the next quarter. Harmony's hedging policy has held back share price performance during the recent sharp rise in gold prices, leaving Harmony trading at a steep discount to peers despite solid operations and record margins. It does feel like the rally in gold is running out of steam here and that the yellow metal might take a pause here before its next leg higher. Therefore, I think Harmony's hedging "penalty" will matter far less over the next quarter—and might even help it if gold takes a dip. I concur with Wall Street analysts that Harmony should see at least 23% further upside from here over the next 12 months.

data.title: Macquarie Small Cap Core Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

During 3Q25, the Fund’s benchmark, the Russell 2000 Index, advanced 12.39% and reached an all-time high. This continued the strongest six-month stretch (April 1, 2025 – September 30, 2025) for the index since the post-Covid rally in late 2020. US small-cap equities outpaced large caps during 3Q25, as the Russell 1000 Index returned 7.99%. In this environment, the smallest market capitalization stocks performed best, with the Russell Microcap Index leading with a 17.03% quarterly return. From a style perspective, small-cap value stocks performed slightly better than growth stocks, with the Russell 2000 Value and the Russell 2000 Growth indices returning 12.60% and 12.19%, respectively. Year-to-date, the Russell 2000 Index is up 10.39%.

US equity markets were positive in each month this quarter but really picked up steam in August following Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s speech at Jackson Hole, which was more dovish than expected. A weaker labor market sparked market expectations for Fed rate cuts, propelling the smallest market capitalization, most highly levered, and lowest-earning companies to perform best. Small cap stock strength continued into September, as the Fed lowered rates by 25 basis points (bps), or 0.25 percentage points, taking the targeted range to 4.0-4.25%. In his speech, Fed Chair Powell indicated labor market risks are beginning to outweigh the risk of higher inflation. The Fed still expects an additional 50 bps of cuts in 2025 and projects the federal funds rate range to fall to 3.0-3.25% by 2027.

In this market environment, sector performance was quite varied. Nine sectors outperformed the Russell 2000 Index, while nine sectors trailed the index. Communication services was the strongest-performing sector, up 42.29%, led by EchoStar Corporation (SATS), which agreed to sell a portion of its wireless spectrum licenses to AT&T (T), and SpaceX (SPACE) which resolved its spectrum utilization issues with the FCC and strengthened its financial position. Capital goods returned more than 20% for the quarter, while basic materials, information technology (IT), and healthcare all rose by more than 15%.

In healthcare, lower interest rates led to strong results for speculative biotechnology companies while medical products stocks were negative for the quarter. Gold and rare earth metals led basic materials higher. In IT, the artificial intelligence (AI) thematic remained strong, as we saw large investments made by Oracle (ORCL), Nvidia (NVDA), and OpenAI (OPENAI) during 3Q. This spurred strong returns for semiconductor companies, technology infrastructure, and power companies leveraged to AI. Sentiment toward small-cap software companies was markedly weak during 3Q on AI disintermediation fears.

Factor analysis plays an important role not only in portfolio construction but also in assessing market performance. Similar to last quarter, MSCI Barra style factor analysis, a multi-factor model that evaluates a security’s risk relative to the market using over 40 data metrics, showed that US equity market performance was predominantly driven by trading and risk factors including beta, momentum, and residual volatility. Meanwhile, earnings quality – which measures cash flows relative to earnings as well as earnings variability – was the weakest-performing factor for the quarter. Historically, following a bear market bottom (a decline of more than 20% from index peak; last occurred on April 8, 2025), low-quality risk factors lead but begin to dissipate in subsequent quarters.

Source: FactSet unless noted otherwise.

Within the Fund

For 3Q25, Macquarie Small Cap Core Institutional Class shares gained, although the Fund underperformed its Russell 2000 Index benchmark. Although stock selection detracted from relative performance, the aforementioned style factors along with industry allocations accounted for most of the underperformance. The portfolio’s overweight to earnings quality resulted in -175 bps of relative performance this quarter and -284 bps year to date; while its underweight to residual volatility has resulted in -134 bps in 3Q and -164 bps year-to-date.

Relative performance results were weakest in IT, basic materials, and capital goods, while stock selection in healthcare contributed most.

Underperformance in IT was a result of weaker stock performance in the software industry as well as limited exposure to quantum computing and cryptocurrency thematics. Software was one of the weakest segments, driven by fears that AI advancement will reduce the difficulty required and need for software engineers to create productive software applications. We believe that these fears are overstated, and historical innovation cycles can provide evidence of that. Additionally, AI is likely to be an advantage to incumbent, enterprise class, cyber secure software providers as much as a threat.

In software, Q2 Holdings, Inc. (QTWO), SPS Commerce, Inc. (SPSC), and Progress Software Corporation (PRGS) were the weakest-performing stocks held in the portfolio, although all three companies continue to execute well. Q2, which provides digital banking services to regional banks that allow them to compete with large, money-center banks, beat estimates and raised guidance for both revenue and earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBTIDA). Management remains confident in its outlook for 16% subscription revenue growth for fiscal year 2025 and continues to deliver substantial margin improvements.

SPS Commerce, which provides cloud-based supply chain management services, lowered its organic growth guidance for 2026 to “at least high single digits” as its target customer base of suppliers tighten their spending on tariff and macroeconomic uncertainty. Despite this, the company continues to deliver exceptional profit margin expansion and improve its customer count, which has been a past concern. We see SPS Commerce as a dominant market leader with a true software-as-a-service (SaaS) product, clean balance sheet, reputable management with consistent guidance, and two-decade track record of solid growth that now trades at depressed valuations.

Semtech Corporation (SMTC) and Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd. (CRDO) were up meaningfully during the quarter. Both companies provide connectivity solutions that enable AI, cloud computing, and hyperscale networks. Semtech, which had some setbacks in 1Q, delivered strong earnings and continues to see robust growth and adoption in its end markets. We initiated a position in Credo Technology amid the Liberation Day uncertainty in early April. The stock has been a strong contributor to absolute performance for the portfolio (+263% in last 6 months).

In capital goods, Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD), which manufactures thermal solutions products used in cooling data centers and other HVAC industrial applications, was a leading contributor. The company raised its fiscal year guidance in anticipation of 45% growth in data center sales. The management team has a strong pedigree and is bringing revenue and margin improvements to the organization. Zurn Elkay Water Solutions Corporation (ZWS) and Chart Industries, Inc. (GTLS) were other solid contributors to results in capital goods. Underperformance in the sector stemmed from a lack of exposure to Bloom Energy Corporation (BE), a manufacturer of fuel-cell-based power generation systems, which returned more than 250% during the quarter.

In finance, insurance broker Baldwin Insurance Group Inc. (BWIN) lagged due to an industry-wide deceleration in organic growth stemming from slowing insurance premium growth and weaker activity levels. Due to its strong competitive positioning, Baldwin is able to deliver organic growth better than the industry, which we believe will be an enduring advantage in the current market environment.

In healthcare, there were several contributors to relative performance, including Ligand Pharmaceuticals Incorporated (LGND), Travere Therapeutics, Inc. (TVTX), Supernus Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (SUPN), Insmed Incorporated (INSM), and Halozyme Therapeutics (HALO). Ligand, which develops and acquires drugs and drug technologies that can be licensed to partner pharmaceutical/biotech companies, saw sizable growth in its royalty outlook largely due to key pipeline approvals and launches over the past year. Supernus, which focuses on treatments for Central Nervous System diseases including approved drugs for epilepsy, saw strong revenue growth in treatments it had acquired in recent years, further diversifying its growth platform.

Our investment process has always been designed around risk mitigation and stock selection. We seek to manage our factor exposure within guardrails to ensure the portfolio is not unintentionally exposed to any risk factor, sector, or macroeconomic variable. Historically, we accomplished this objective by adjusting individual weights of stocks held in our portfolio. Currently, the market’s focus on thematics and the extraordinary effect of some factors such as beta and residual volatility has made this difficult to achieve organically within the existing holdings in our portfolio. To improve our risk management, during the 3Q, we added several small positions in names that brought our risk exposures more in line with our historical bounds. While this decision was a net positive to 3Q performance, it did not outweigh the overall negative headwind from factors in the quarter.

Outlook

April 1, 2025, through September 30, 2025, has been an unprecedented six-month period for small-cap equities and our portfolio. While we are encouraged by recent strength in the asset class, the narrowness in thematics and speculative trading activity causes concern. We have experienced similar risk rallies coming out of previous bear markets (for example, global financial crisis, Covid). These are typical market environments in which our higher-quality investment style underperforms. It can be painful. Yet, history has shown that these market factors tend to be transitory and eventually fundamentals prevail. This is not our weakest six-month period, but it is certainly among the top in our 20-year history. We are displeased with the recent results but not discouraged. Finding and investing in companies with attractive business models and managers who are good stewards of capital remains our priority and focus and is what has contributed to our consistent long-term results.

With respect to sector positioning, finance remains the largest sector in the Fund and represents our largest relative overweight. Within finance, we maintain our overweight to regional banks due to an improving earnings profile and a supportive regulatory environment, which we think should encourage credit growth and M&A activity. We continue to monitor developments in economic conditions since that is a key factor for bank profitability.

Consumer discretionary and consumer staples represent our next largest sector overweights relative to the benchmark. In consumer staples, we purchased Chefs' Warehouse, Inc. (CHEF), which distributes specialty foods to fine dining and polished casual independent restaurants. Compared with large broadline distributors, Chef’s Warehouse offers greater breadth and depth of specialty food products and high-touch customer service. After a very active period of acquisitions and capacity expansion coming out of the pandemic, CHEF has been focused recently on integrating acquisitions, improving margins, reducing capital expenses (capex), de-leveraging the balance sheet, and returning more capital to shareholders. Management has outlined a credible plan to grow EBITDA and expand margins over the next four years. The company is also more exposed to a more affluent client, those who benefit from positive wealth effects, outsized tax benefits, and who are more insulated from inflation compared to lower income consumer.

We are most underweight in IT, which we believe has a high prevalence of less mature companies, lofty expectations on AI capex, and/or unproven business models. Within IT, we are overweight semiconductors and software, with a focus on stock-specific outcomes.

We continue to believe that small-cap stocks remain attractive due to strong earnings growth estimates, further Fed rate cuts, and stimulative government policies encouraging capex, domestic manufacturing, and deregulation. The forward price/earnings ratio for the Russell 2000 Index stands at a multiple of 15.9, as compared with the Russell 1000 Index at a multiple of 23.5. Additionally, earnings growth is expected to improve over the coming year with small caps earnings growing faster than large caps. That would provide small caps with a much-needed fundamental catalyst. At the time of writing, FactSet estimates 2026 earnings per share (EPS) growth of 41.7% for Russell 2000 versus Russell 1000 estimates of 13.9%. Despite a supportive backdrop, we do anticipate volatility and choppiness, as several risk factors remain prevalent, including a weakening labor market, tariff implications, the government shutdown, and lack of economic growth outside of AI spending. We believe quality should eventually shine through in this type of environment.

data.title: TSP Roth Conversion Option Coming In 2026: What Federal Employees And Retirees Need To Know
data.description:
Starting January 1, 2026, Federal employees and retirees will be able to convert money from their traditional Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) accounts to a Roth TSP account. That is called a Roth in-plan conversion. Read the TSP announcement here.

This new feature gives TSP participants a tool that’s long been available for Roth IRAs but has been missing from the TSP: the ability to manage taxes proactively by shifting money into a tax-free growth vehicle. You don’t need an existing Roth TSP balance to do it — your first in-plan conversion will create one.

But just because you can convert doesn’t mean you should. That is because any amounts converted are fully taxable as ordinary income for the year they are made. TSP participants who convert are paying taxes early in order to reduce their future taxes. As a result, conversions will make sense for some TSP participants but not others.

The Difference Between Traditional and Roth TSP Accounts

Federal employees can make their bi-weekly contribution to two types of TSP accounts – traditional (also called regular) and Roth. The government’s matching contributions always go to the employee’s traditional TSP account.

  • Traditional TSP:

Employee contributions to the Traditional TSP are pre-tax. That means the employee’s taxable income is reduced by the amount of the contribution. Earnings generated by the TSP funds are not taxed while the contributions remain in the TSP. However, withdrawals from the Traditional TSP are fully taxable as ordinary income.

  • Roth TSP:

Employee contributions to the Roth TSP are after tax; employees cannot deduct Roth contributions from their taxable income when calculating their taxes. However, Qualified withdrawals are tax-free and there are no Required Minimum Distributions.

For a withdrawal to be qualified, the participant must be older than 59 ½ (or permanently disabled) and the Roth TSP account must be more than five years old.

The only disadvantage to contributing to the Roth TSP is that the contribution is not tax deductible; it cannot be used to reduce your taxable income when calculating your tax payment. Contributions are “after-tax.”

In other respects, it is better to have money in the Roth than Traditional TSP:

  • There are no Required Minimum Distributions
  • Withdrawals are not taxed if qualified.
  • Spousal beneficiaries and heirs do not have to pay tax on withdrawals.

Currently, neither employees nor retirees can convert traditional TSP balances to a Roth TSP account. Starting January 1, 2026, both employees and retirees can convert any amount to a Roth TSP account.

How Roth In-Plan Conversions Will Work

Beginning in 2026, employees and retirees can instruct the TSP to transfer funds from their traditional TSP account to a Roth TSP account. The amount of the transfer is considered taxable income for that tax year.

Note that

  • There is a $500 minimum for each separate conversion.
  • The entire amount converted is treated as taxable income.
  • The TSP will not withhold taxes on conversions.
  • The TSP will report the taxable income to the IRS.
  • The taxes must be paid with funds from a savings or taxable investment account.
  • You cannot pay taxes using funds from the TSP or an IRA unless you first withdraw the money and transfer to a taxable account, generating additional taxable income.
  • The converted balance remains in the TSP, invested in the same funds with the same percentage allocation.

IMPORTANT: There are a lot of variables that affect whether a Roth conversion will be a good strategy.

Plan ahead. If you don’t have funds in a taxable account to pay the taxes, a conversion may not make sense.

When Roth Conversions Might Make Sense

A conversion might make sense when a TSP participant:

  • Expects their tax rate to be higher in retirement. If the participant's tax rate in retirement is expected to be lower or the same, a conversion probably does not make sense.
  • Has cash on hand to pay the conversion tax.
  • Wants to reduce future RMDs from their traditional TSP account.
  • Wants to leave tax-free money to heirs.
  • Is an aggressive investor expecting high growth — the bigger the gains, the more benefit to tax-free treatment.
  • Does not expect the funds in the Roth account to be needed for a long period of time.
  • Want to lower Medicare premiums by reducing taxes on future withdrawals.

A conversion may not make sense when a TSP participant:

  • Is currently in a high tax bracket.
  • Is subject to the Net Investment Income Tax.
  • Needs the money within the next 5–10 years. Conversions take time to pay off.
  • Is 75+ and already taking RMDs.
  • Doesn’t have funds outside the TSP to pay the taxes.

Mathematically, if a participant expects their tax rates to be lower or the same in retirement, a Roth conversion is probably not beneficial. But for participants who expect their retirement tax rate to be higher, it could pay off — especially for long-term, growth-oriented investors.

Final Thoughts: Who Should Consider This?

Congratulations to the TSP for offering another option. But this option is not for everyone; it can backfire without careful planning. Participants should ask a financial planner or tax advisor to help them decide whether it makes sense. The TSP has wisely emphasized this. The wrong decision can cost more in taxes than it saves and reduce the future after-tax value of investments.
