data.title: Blue Owl Capital Corporation: Buying 12%+ Yield As AI Excitement Fades
data.description:
Despite strong Q3 earnings delivered by the largest tech companies with AI exposure, we see that the rally across the most prominent names has stopped. This might indicate that the market's excitement regarding AI is moderating, which means that some investors would start rotating to deep value. In this respect, Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) looks compelling with its forward 12%+ dividend yield. Moreover, the stock is significantly undervalued after share price weakness since my previous coverage in December 2024. The payout ratio is quite high, which might limit dividend growth potential. However, I think that a 12%+ dividend yield is too attractive to ignore, even considering future dividend growth risks. To diversify my heavily growth-oriented portfolio, I am loading up OBDC and reiterating 'Strong Buy.'

Fundamental Analysis

Let me first start with explanation why I think that there might be a notable rotation from growth to value over the next few months. The Q3 earnings season revealed that the largest tech companies are increasing their AI infrastructure budgets, which made investors very nervous because such an aggressive capex increase announced by all prominent players raise concerns about computing capacity oversupply in the future. As a result, even Nvidia's recent strong Q3 earnings release did not trigger a new wave of market excitement. As a result, we see that stocks of almost all the largest companies with AI exposure demonstrated relatively weak performance after their latest earnings releases.

Probably the biggest AI darling in terms of share price growth over the last couple of years, Palantir (PLTR), has also tumbled despite massive growth across all vital metrics during the latest reportable quarter. Even strong earnings revisions record over the last month did not protect PLTR from a quite significant share price drop since November 3. I think that quite weak share price performance demonstrated by the biggest AI beneficiaries after Q3 earnings releases likely indicates that the market's excitement regarding AI potential is somewhere near peak levels.

Finally, deteriorating sentiment around growth stocks is also likely caused by the high average P/E ratio for the broader market. According to Current Market Valuation, the average P/E ratio of S&P500 is not at the dot-com bubble levels, but is very close to late 2021 levels. We all remember that most of the largest tech stocks suffered in 2022 during high inflation and soaring energy prices. While inflation is currently much closer to historical level of 2%, there are other uncertainties in the macro environment including gradually rising unemployment rate in the U.S., continued trade war with China, and the Fed's next steps regarding interest rates. Therefore, the demand for safe harbor instruments is likely to rise as investors might be willing to protect their growth-heavy portfolios.

With such a setup, OBDC's 12%+ forward dividend yield looks like a compelling income play. There are some concerns about future dividend growth, which I will discuss later in this article. However, even with potentially low dividend growth, the base of 12%+ is already very attractive. And I think that the current level of dividend is safe because of OBDC's robust business mix which consists of lending to middle-market enterprises, real estate, and equity investments across 238 portfolio companies. Of course, the lion's portion of the portfolio is represented by first-lien debt investments (around 75%, according to the pie chart). However, within this category, there is another level of diversification as borrowers represent dozens of different industries. Thus, the extensive and multi-layer diversification significantly mitigates concentration risks, which is likely to support dividend stability over the long term.

Such diversification helps OBDC to support stable financial performance with robust profitability compared to industry averages. Robust profitability is vital for dividend safety as it helps to support the balance sheet, which currently boasts a $3.2 billion liquidity in form of cash and undrawn debt capacity. The company's permanent capital base and long-term management contracts provide stability for its cash flow. Additionally, the company has demonstrated access to capital markets, which could provide additional financial flexibility if needed. By the way, the above screenshot shows us that OBDC's book value per share was slightly below $15 as of the end of Q3, while its current share price is below $13. Let me proceed with valuation analysis in the next section of my analysis.

Valuation Analysis

I am buying OBDC to get hefty dividend yield. Therefore, to understand whether its valuation is compelling, I used the dividend discount model ('DDM') approach. Since the risk-free rate remains quite high at the moment, I prefer to use the same 8.8% discount rate as I did in my previous analysis.

With the $1.56 current dividend and a 2% constant growth rate assumptions, OBDC's fair value is circa $23. It is significantly higher than the current share price of around $13. The gap is wide, and such a deep discount might be due to concerns about OBDC's ability to drive a 2% dividend growth rate. Therefore, I would also like to simulate a scenario with a very subtle dividend growth of 0.5%.

As we can see, even with such a low dividend growth rate assumption, the fair value is still around 44% above the last close. Therefore, I think that OBDC is significantly undervalued.

Mitigating Factors

As I mentioned in valuation, there might be concerns that OBDC will be unable to deliver a 2% dividend growth rate over the long term. These concerns are not unfair because of OBDC's high dividend payout ratios. As readers can see from the table below, OBDC distributes quite high percentage of its profits as dividends. Since any company needs spare funds to finance strategic growth initiatives, high payout ratios might weigh on OBDC's dividend growth potential. I continue investing in OBDC despite high payout ratios because a 12% dividend yield is still very attractive for me even if there is no dividend growth in the future. However, this approach might not fit some investors who prefer growth over yield.

Blue Owl's prosperity as an alternative asset manager depends on overall market conditions and financial cycles. A prolonged downturn or a significant market adjustment would affect the company's capacity to secure capital, invest resources, and generate profits. This could lead to reduced management fees, decreased incentive income, and strain on the company's dividend.

Conclusion

With expectations around AI appear to be approaching peak levels, I think that adding more shares of OBDC to my portfolio is a sound decision. The stock is significantly undervalued and offers a 12%+ forward dividend yield, which looks like a perfect deep value play for my heavily growth-oriented portfolio.

data.title: Magna Mining: The Real Story Is Forming Underground, Not In The Quarter
data.description:
There are companies that you do not understand in a quick reading, neither in a balance sheet nor in a quarter. And that’s exactly what happened to me when I started analyzing Magna Mining (OTCQB:MGMNF) (NICU:CA). What led me to cover it was not a point, but the feeling of being in front of a company that is still finding its way in a district where mining is not only a business, but part of the local identity.

The first thing that struck me was not the company itself, but the place where it operates. Sudbury is not just another mining district; it’s almost a world of its own. When you read "more than a century of production" it may sound cold, but when you start to see what it means that an entire community has lived for so many decades around mining, the look changes. Here there are shafts that exist since before many companies in the sector were born, infrastructure that is already installed, people who understand the craft from childhood and an underground knowledge that is not learned in any book

That was when I decided to analyze in more detail, because a young company that enters such a district does not start from scratch: it enters into something that is already in motion. And that changes a lot. It does not imply guarantee of success, but we know that the context matters, and in mining it matters even more than in almost any other sector.

With all this in mind, I looked at Magna again from another angle, and this time I saw it differently. McCreedy West is not a "more" mine; it’s the way the company holds its present as it tries to sort itself out. And Levack is also not a futuristic project: it is, rather, the way to try to climb within that huge ecosystem that is Sudbury. One holds, the other promises. And though they are far from perfect, they are real, and that says something.

I also think it’s worth mentioning that while Magna today is not what it wants to be, it’s not just an idea. It is at that midpoint where the company is trying to turn geology into economics. And that kind of moment, as uncomfortable as it sometimes is, are usually the most interesting to analyze. Because that is when the stories appear that later the market discovers when it is too late.

An Uncomfortable but Revealing Quarter

Going into the numbers, the first thing I have to say is that Q3 2025 was not an orderly quarter, but quite the opposite. The operation progressed as best it could while dealing with frictions that anyone who has worked near an underground mine instantly recognizes. As mentioned by Magna, there were failures in the compressed air system, electrical delays and those times that get stretched when you try to reach the stope that really matters. In that context, McCreedy West ended up processing 75,215 tons and producing 2.7MM of CuEq lbs, but it did so bearing the weight of those delays that disrupted the entire operational pace.

At the same time, below the surface, the company was advancing nearly 1,800 feet of development and investing $4.1MM in equipment, rehabilitation and infrastructure. It’s the kind of spending that doesn’t make a quarter look good, but we know it defines what the next year can look like. And for companies in transition, those kinds of decisions matter even more than the one-off result.

However, the most relevant data was not within the quarter, but just after. In November, the company finally accessed major stopes. They arrived late to improve the photo of Q3, yes, but they did so in time to start changing the operational direction towards 2026. This, to me, speaks more of the future than of the quarter itself.

So, from my point of view, it was not a great quarter, but one that helps us understand what was going on behind the number. And sometimes, in stages like this, that’s more important than the final metric.

Liquidity as a Margin to Think

There is something I always look at in companies of this size, and it’s not just how much they produce, but how much time they have to solve what they need to solve. In companies in transition, "time" is almost an asset in itself. That’s why I came back to the liquidity of Magna, which closed the quarter with $63.1MM in cash and, after the exercise of warrants, ended with a total of $69.1MM.

From there, I made a simple but useful calculation: the operating cash flow of Q3 was negative at $10.8MM. If you take that rate as a reference (understanding that mines never spend the same every quarter) the cash runway is around 6 quarters, something like a year and a half.

This, for me, changes the reading, because in a sector where many companies have to issue shares every six months just to keep breathing, the fact that Magna has something as basic as "time" gives it air. It doesn’t mean you can relax, but it does mean it can explore, develop and reach the Levack PEA without relying on a perfect funding window.

In other words, while liquidity does not solve the future of the company, it gives Magna a margin. And at this stage, having margin is what allows a company to make learning mistakes without compromising continuity.

Levack: The place where you can change everything

When the Mineral Resource Estimate came out I felt that Levack was no longer an old, passive asset but something with a real possibility to move the needle. Not because the numbers alone are spectacular, but because they show that there is a concrete starting point, something to build on.

The first thing that caught my eye was the Morrison Footwall. I do not need to be a geologist to understand that 178,000 tons indicated with a very high grade of 15.5% CuEq is something that does not appear every day, and much less in a district where mining has been going on for more than a century. When a deposit so worked still holds metal at that grade, what it suggests is not luck, but economic potential.

And then R2 appeared. Here, more than a formal discovery, we are talking about a signal. Drilling begins to show interesting mineralization in the midpoint, as if there were something larger connecting sectors that were previously seen as independent. There is no need to go into technicalities, but the relevant thing is that these results, if they get repeated, can open a new front that today is not in the share price.

The technical team summarized it as follows:

We are encouraged by the assay results received from our third drillhole testing the R2 exploration target as we continue to intersect high grade, precious metal rich massive sulfide veins in the footwall environment of the Levack Mine.

It sounds a little technical, but translated into the language of someone who thinks about value and not geology, this is like: "we keep finding material that could be economically very interesting".

For those who want to look at the technical presentation, the following 3D chart helps to understand the idea. Morrison is located below, the more classic areas above, and R2 right in the middle, where unexpected opportunities often appear. I look at it less from the geology and more from the potential, and this tells me that if these bodies become connected or show continuity, it changes the scale of the project.

This naturally raises a question, which I want to answer with all sincerity. The first is if it’s safe, and the answer is "no", which brings me to the next, and is if it’s enough to start paying attention to Levack as a potential catalyst for 2026, here the answer changes to "yes". In companies of this scale, you don’t need many discoveries, and one that is good can change the story entirely.

Risks

When I analyze the risks, I see that they are not concentrated in a single point, but spread throughout the path that Magna is trying to build. The first is in McCreedy West, because the operation has not yet found a stable pace, the costs are still high and any technical hiccups are too noticeable in a company of this size. That is why I think that until the mine finds stability, the quarters will continue to show this irregularity.

The second risk, for me, is Levack. While it's clear that the potential is there, the economics of the project still don't exist. The 2026 PEA will be the document that tells how it can be accessed, how much it costs and whether the deposit can actually sustain a profitable operation. And while intersections may excite us, what defines value is continuity, size, and cost of access. And those are still unknowns.

To this I add a logical financial risk. Although Magna has the cash to reach the PEA without shocks, the burn rate is negative, and the base metals market does not always accompany it. After the PEA it probably needs capital, and how much the company dilutes will depend on how solid that study is.

And the last risk I see, which at first glance is the simplest but actually is the most complex, is time. In mining, nothing moves in a straight line. And although it may seem a minor detail, in the end it is that time which ends up defining the rhythm of a project, especially one of this nature.

Conclusion

After analyzing all the information, reviewing the quarter, the cash, the geology and the pace at which the company is progressing, I arrived at a position that is neither extreme nor definitive. My position, for now, is a Hold until PEA results. Not because it lacks potential, but because the story is entering a stage where geology has already said what it had to say and now it’s time for the economics of the project to speak.

While it's possible to recognize that interesting things are happening underground and that the company is moving in the right direction, it still needs a solid economic framework to become a well-founded Buy. When that PEA arrives, then and only then will we decide if we are facing the beginning of a larger story or looking at a project that needs more time to mature.
